coredata.dc:title: Carbon dots from agroindustrial residues: a critical comparison of the effect of physicochemical properties on their performance as photocatalyst and emulsion stabilizer

Mercado, D.F.
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots have been synthesized through a pyrolysis route with agroindustrial wastes of Yerba Mate (YM), Avocado Seed (AS), and Orange Peel (OP) as raw materials. The selection of the pyrolysis temperature was according to the thermogravimetric profile of the raw materials. A thorough characterization by transmission electron microscopy (TEM), ζ-potential, ultraviolet visible spectroscopy (UV/vis), Raman, Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy, and steady-state, and time-resolved area normalized emission spectroscopy (TRANES) suggest spherical nanomaterials with diameters lower than 5.0 nm and polyfunctional surfaces with an abundance of negatively charged surface groups. Regarding their application as photocatalyst and as Pickering emulsion stabilizers, all materials have different performances. In the first case, almost 100% and 40% of the photodecoloration of methylene blue was achieved in 2 h by the materials obtained from YM and AS, respectively. Nevertheless, different mechanisms were proposed for both cases. Regarding their performance as Pickering emulsion stabilizers, all materials show different performances. Interestingly, Raman spectroscopy results provide useful information to understand the observed results.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots have been synthesized through a pyrolysis route with agroindustrial wastes of Yerba Mate (YM), Avocado Seed (AS), and Orange Peel (OP) as raw materials. The selection of the pyrolysis temperature was according to the thermogravimetric profile of the raw materials. A thorough characterization by transmission electron microscopy (TEM), ζ-potential, ultraviolet visible spectroscopy (UV/vis), Raman, Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy, and steady-state, and time-resolved area normalized emission spectroscopy (TRANES) suggest spherical nanomaterials with diameters lower than 5.0 nm and polyfunctional surfaces with an abundance of negatively charged surface groups. Regarding their application as photocatalyst and as Pickering emulsion stabilizers, all materials have different performances. In the first case, almost 100% and 40% of the photodecoloration of methylene blue was achieved in 2 h by the materials obtained from YM and AS, respectively. Nevertheless, different mechanisms were proposed for both cases. Regarding their performance as Pickering emulsion stabilizers, all materials show different performances. Interestingly, Raman spectroscopy results provide useful information to understand the observed results.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CD) are a relatively new nanomaterial, discovered in 2004 as by-products in single-walled carbon nanotubes (SWCNTs) synthesis [1]. They have gained attention because of their remarkable properties and relatively simple and low-cost synthesis methods. Typically, they have a quasispherical geometry with a diameter lower than 10 nm and depending on their crystallinity; they present both sp3 and sp2 carbon moieties in their structure [2]. Being some of their advantages, a low toxicity, high biocompatibility, strong photoluminescence, and optical stability characterize the carbon dots [3]. Its surface can be composed of different functional groups, depending on the synthesis route and precursors used, among them: carboxyl, hydroxyl, epoxy, aldehyde, amines, and amides [4]. These groups would result in a surface with high charge and hydrophilicity that facilitate the formation of well-dispersed aqueous suspensions [5].

Nevertheless, the physicochemical properties, i.e. the potential application fields of the obtained materials, might depend on the composition of the used raw materials. Interestingly, CD could be obtained not only from pure molecules but also from vegetal biomasses [6–10]. As of the date, typically, the studies report using just one biomass to obtain CD with a specific application, but therefore it is not completely clear how the different physicochemical properties affect the CD's performance on the studied applications and potential applications based on the unique characteristics of each material that is wasted [5,11–14]. CD are typically applied on optoelectronics, structure reinforcement [4], energy conversion [5], biomedicine, sensors and biosensors [2,15], wastewater treatment, catalysis [16], and as Pickering emulsion stabilizer [17]. The two last applications are of interest due to the CD's distinct photoluminescent properties and dispersibility in water.

In the first place, heterogeneous photocatalysis is an advanced oxidation process widely used in water remediation treatments, based on the reaction assisted by photons in the presence of a photoactive material acting as a semiconductor, which produces the promotion from the filled valence band (VB) to the empty conduction band (CB), generating an electron-hole (e−-h+) pair system [5,18]. The minimum required energy for this process is determined by the Band-gap of the semiconductor [19]. The (e−-h+) pair might react with dissolved molecules producing oxidant species such as HO• (2.80 V vs. normal hydrogen electrode [NHE], 25 °C) and O2 •− (1.8 V vs. NHE, 25 °C), which can oxidize almost all organic molecules even to mineralization. [20,21], Typically, semiconductors employed metal-based materials (commonly TiO2 based materials) as photocatalysts; nevertheless, these semiconductors would be toxic, expensive, difficult to synthesize, or due to their relatively high bandgap energy, there are photoactive on the UV region, which results in the wasting of the majority of the solar irradiation [22].

As semiconductors, CDs present advantages as non-toxic, reutilize, inexpensive, and easy to synthesize materials, with typically low bandgap energies that allow, in some cases, solar photocatalyst applications [23,24]. Additionally, the CD surface chemistry can change depending on the raw material, modifying the material's photochemical properties and subsequently their application as a catalyst in different systems due to processes that enhance the electronic transfers [22]. In fact, in the literature, the use of CD has been extensively studied for this purpose [2,18,25]. Nevertheless, these studies typically do not consider the raw material impact on the CD photocatalytic performance. That is why the study of physicochemical properties in the CD can clarify the relationship between the mechanism of catalyst and the chemical disposition in their structure and allow the design of more efficient CD in the future.

In parallel, an emulsion is a heterogeneous system that consists of at least one immiscible liquid, the dispersed phase, forming droplets in another, the continuum phase. Emulsions have applications in many different fields, including drug delivery [26], cosmetics [27], food [28], petroleum industry [29], among others [30]. Commonly, they are stabilized by molecules soluble in both liquids, called surfactants, which due to their amphiphilic nature, i.e. their structure has both lipophilic and hydrophilic segments, interact with both phases of the emulsion, thus decreasing the interfacial surface tension of the system [31]. Nevertheless, surfactants might show adverse effects on the environment and human health due to toxic, mutagenic, or carcinogenic properties [32–34].

Thus, the use of solid surfactants to stabilize emulsion has gained attention due to their cost-efficiency and low toxicity [35]. An emulsion stabilized by a solid surfactant is called a Pickering emulsion. Several materials, such as clays, hydroxyapatite, graphene oxide [35], silica [36], chitosan [37], magnetite-coated polystyrene nanoparticles [38], all with characteristic lengths larger than 10 nm, have been successfully used to stabilize Pickering emulsions. However, the synthesis protocols for obtaining these materials are complicated and are time-demanding. Furthermore, the final droplet sizes depend on the individual particle sizes; thus, smaller particles are interested in producing emulsions with smaller droplet sizes [32,39,40].

Hence, the use of nanomaterials with all dimensions lower than 10 nm would facilitate the obtaining of emulsions with smaller droplet sizes [17]. The use of CD as Pickering emulsion stabilizers have already been reported [12,17,26]; nevertheless, these studies are focused on the impact of several formulation parameters; mixing energy and time, water percentage, CD concentration, etcetera, on the droplet sizes and emulsion rheology. However, the CD's physicochemical properties' impact on the droplet size distribution is not yet clear.

Several synthesis routes have been proposed, through bottom-up and top-down approaches: microwaves and ultrasonic routes, thermal decomposition, laser ablation, hydrothermal, plasma treatments, chemical, and electrochemical oxidation [2,3]. The CD obtained from biomass initially has the same application fields regarding the ones obtained from pure reagents, but due to agroindustrial residues' potential use as precursors, they are economically more attractive and their surface can possess some residual functional groups that would enhance the properties of the CD [3]. Cultivation of Ilex paraguariensis, commonly known as Yerba Mate (YM), represents an important economic activity in several South American countries. YM leaf extracts are intensively consumed as infusions and used in the formulation of foods. We have reported the advantages of using YM residues as surface coatings agents for magnetite nanoparticles [41,42]. In parallel, orange and avocado are some of the world's most commercialized tropical fruits. The biowastes produced from their consumption, such as orange peel (OP) and avocado seeds (AS), are interesting bioplatforms to obtain chemical products and (nano)materials [43], thus facilitating the integration into a circular economy [44–46]. [47,48] Because of their chemical composition, they are of interest as precursors in CD synthesis [49].

This study reports CD's obtaining from YM, AS, and OP through a facile pyrolysis route, finding the optimal temperature for the conversion of cellulose and hemicellulose based compounds into CD. The pyrolysis temperature selection was supported by a detailed analysis of the thermogravimetric profiles of the raw materials. All particles were characterized, and two potential applications in the liquid phase were considered: photocatalysts in a dye photodecoloration and (Pickering) emulsion stabilization. Finally, a correlation between the physicochemical properties of CD and their potential applications, using the performances of all synthesized materials either as photocatalysts or as emulsion stabilizers as examples of the application proposed prediction is discussed.

Sodium hydroxide (NaOH) 99% wt, potassium hydroxide (KOH) 99% wt, sulfuric acid (H2SO4) 97% wt, nitric acid (HNO3) 65%, potassium nitrate (KNO3) 99% wt sodium azide (NaN3) 99.5% wt, furfuryl alcohol (FA), hydrogen peroxide solution (H2O2) 30% v/v, methylene blue <96% wt and bromobenzene 99% wt were provided by Merck. The commercial enzymatic colorimetric kit was provided by the Wiener Laboratorios S.A.I.C., Rosario, Argentina. All were used without further purification. YM herein used were from the commercial trademark El Playadito, Liebig, Farming Cooperative, Argentina, production lot 45-03. OP and AS were obtained from the public market in Bucaramanga, Colombia. All solids were ground and sieved (<150 μm). Deionized water was Milli-Q purified (18 MΩ cm and 6 ppb TOC at 25 °C).

For selecting the pyrolysis temperature, thermogravimetric analyses (TGA) were recorded using a Q600 TA Instruments interfaced with a computer. 10 mg of the corresponding sample were maintained at 50 °C for 5 min, and then heated at 10 °C/min up to 700 °C. All experiments were carried out in an N2 atmosphere with a flow of 50 mL/min.

For CD obtaining, 10 g of the respective raw material was introduced in a closed crucible and heated from room temperature to 400 °C (vide infra) with a ramp of 10 °C/min. Then the system was maintained invariant for 2 h. Once the obtained coal was at room temperature, 0.03 g of the powder were suspended in 10 mL of water and left under ultrasound agitation for 1 h at (27°±°1) °C. The solid was then separated from the supernatant by filtration with hydrophobic membranes (ø = 0.1 μm) and the yellow-pale suspension was subjected to centrifugation at 13000 rpm for 15 min to remove the bulk particles. The final suspension was stored at 4 °C. The obtained CDs were denoted as CD-X, where X corresponds to YM, OP, or AS, referring to the used agroindustrial residue.

The particles' morphology and size were studied using transmission electron microscopy (TEM) on a Tecnai F20 super twin TMP, with a field emission of 0.1 nm in 200 Kv, equipped with a GATAN US 1000XP-P camera. The GATAN Digital Micrograph software was used to analyze the obtained micrographs [50].

IR spectra were obtained with a Nicolet 380 spectrophotometer working at 128 scans with 2 cm−1 resolution in the range 4000–400 cm−1. Samples were prepared by suspending dried KBr on an aqueous suspension of the desired CD for 24 h. The solid was separated by centrifugation (400 rpm) and then heated at 60 °C for 12 h. The obtained powder was pressed to a load of 5 tons and further analyzed.

Raman spectra were collected by using a Renishaw Ramascope instrument equipped with a 10 mW 532 nm laser (SpectraPhysics) working at 10% power with a 30 s acquisition time, a Rayleigh filter, a 1200 line/mm grating, and a SYNAPSE CCD model 354010 (Horiba) detector.

The Z potential (ζ) profiles over the pH for all materials were measured by Electrophoretic Light Scattering (ELS) using a LiteSizer 500 by Anton Paar coupled to a Metrohm automatic titrator with an 867-pH module and an 846-dosing interface controlled by the software Kalliope™. The setup considered aqueous suspensions of the corresponding material (0.15% w/w) with KNO3 as the background electrolyte. The temperature was (25.0 ± 0.1) °C, the pH was varied from 11 to 3 by steps of 0.5 with 0.01 M of HNO3 or KOH and stabilization time of 5 min per step. Each measurement was done in triplicate. The Smoluchowski approximation method was used.

Fluorescence experiments were carried out in a HORIBA-YVON Spex Fluorolog FL3-11, applying the time-correlated single-photon counting (TCSPC) technique using a quartz cell with 10 mm path length. All experiments were performed at (25.0 ± 0.1) °C controlled with an F-3004 Peltier sample cooler controlled by an LFI-3751 temperature controller (Wavelength electronics). The excitation source was a 450 W Xenon lamp and monochromated with a 1 nm bandpass gap for selecting excitation and emission wavelength for steady irradiation. Emitted photons were registered at 90° with respect to the incident beam using a room-temperature R928P detector. Raw emission spectra were corrected for Raman scattering by subtracting the pure solvent spectrum. For the estimation of the fluorescence quantum yields (Φfl), all experiments were carried out for diluted optical samples (A<0.1) at identical conditions and compared to a 0.5 M quinine sulfate in a sulfuric acid solution used as a reference (Φfl = 0.546 ± 0.02) [51]. Eq. (1) shows the used correlation:

where; A is the absorbance at the excitation wavelength (350 nm), I is the fluorescence intensity at the emission wavelength, Φfl is the luminescence quantum yield under the studied conditions, n is the refractive index of the pure solvent, r and s correspond to the reference and sample respectively.

Steady-state generation and quenching of 1O2 experiments involved the irradiation with a Rayonet lamp emitting at (350 ± 20) nm of air-saturated aqueous suspensions of the particles contained in a closed reactor. Irradiation experiments were performed in the presence and absence of 1O2 scavengers: furfuryl alcohol (FA) and sodium azide (NaN3) [52]. The oxygen concentration was measured throughout the reaction time with an oxygen-sensitive electrode (Orion97-0899).

Indirect evidence of superoxide anion radical (O2 .-) photoproduction was achieved using an enzymatic colorimetric method employing a Colestat commercial kit for cholesterol to quantify H2O2 concentration. Calibration curves were performed using standard H2O2.

The CD's photocatalytic activity was studied by promoting the degradation of methylene blue (MB) at room temperature in batch experiments. Different volumes of each CD suspension were added to a specific beaker containing pure water to obtain a CD suspensions with the same absorbance at the excitation wavelength (λex = 350 ± 20 nm) and with a final volume of 100 mL. Then, the dye's powder was added to obtain 5 mg/L, and the suspensions were stirred for 2 h in the dark to guarantee the adsorption-desorption equilibrium. The excitation source was the Rayonet lamp previously mentioned. The photodecoloration of the system was measured by Uv/vis. To elucidate the effect of 1O2 on MB photodegradation, the same conditions were used in experiments in the presence of FA and NaN3. In parallel, for O2 .- nitrotetrazolium blue chloride and ethanol were used.

All materials were used as emulsion stabilizers using bromobenzene as oil phase and water (50% v/v and pH 7.0) in glass test tubes. The CD-aqueous suspensions were agitated using an ultrasonic bath, working at 37 kHz for 15 min and immediately after the oil phase was added to the system and then sonicated for 15 min more.
Mean droplet sizes were obtained from the three emulsions using a LiteSizer 500 by Anton Paar, working at 90° and (25.0 ± 0.1)°C. Samples were analyzed in triplicate.

The pyrolysis temperature influences the luminescence properties of the obtained CD [53]. However, the ‵optimal′ synthesis temperature for obtaining CD depends on the respective raw materials′ thermogravimetric profiles. In that regard, all agroindustrial residues have the typical thermal decomposition profile for vegetal biomass [54], depicted in the TGA curves shown in Fig. 1 . For temperatures lower than 200 °C, the slight decay of the biomass weight, typically lower than 10%, is due to moisture and the loss of volatile compounds. A major mass loss is observed for temperatures between 200 and 500 °C due to hemicellulose, cellulose, and lignin decomposition. Typically, hemicellulose's thermal decomposition occurs between 150 and 350 °C, cellulose in the range 375–350 °C, and lignin between 250 and 500 °C. As shown in the same figure, the corresponding first derivative of the TGA curve shows at least two distinguishable peaks with positions and intensity depending on the sample's chemical composition. All the particles show a peak between 250 and 350 °C assigned to the thermal decomposition of hemicellulose and cellulose-like compounds. Specifically, for YM, the first derivate shows a peak at 304 °C; however, see Fig. 1 inset, the presence of two inflection points at 299 and 311 °C are indicative of hemicellulose and cellulose oxidation, respectively. These processes seem to occur between 213 and 309 °C for OP, which agrees with the previous reports [55], and between 167° and 294 °C for AS.

Lignin degradation is evidenced by the peaks at 440, 460, and 456 °C for YM, OP, and AS, respectively. Temperatures higher than 500 °C promote total oxidation of the organic matter, leading to the respective ashes' formation. Hence, TGA analysis suggests a possible synthesis temperature range between 350 and 420 °C for all raw materials. Thus 400 °C was selected as the pyrolysis temperature for the three precursors.

Fig. 2 shows some images of the obtained materials and their fluorescent behavior, as well as the TEM micrographs of all samples. The presence of fluorescence in the samples is the initial evidence of the possible formation of CD, the mechanism of this phenomenon is already unknown but has been related by some authors, among others to the CD to the particle size, surface functional groups or heteroatoms, and synthesis methods [56–58].

Regarding the TEM micrographs, it is evident that all materials are in the nanometric scale with spherical geometry, and no crystalline structure can be observed. It was possible to estimate the individual size distribution with a statistical population of at least 100, findings in all cases of particle sizes less than 10 nm, confirming the formation of CD [59]. Specifically for CD-YM, CD-OP, and CD-AS, the mean particle size is (5.1 ± 1.5), (2.8 ± 0.8), and (4.2 ± 1.5) nm, respectively. Figure. S1 shown a detailed micrograph of each material, marking some particles with different sizes measured in the size distribution diagrams, in these images, we can note ‵amorphous′ structures, larger than those considered in the diagrams, and because of one of the characteristics on CD is their rounded shape [3,56,57,60], and appreciable differences in the morphology, we considered these larger structures as agglomerates of individual CD, marking with dotted circles the subunits of the coalesced nanoparticles.

In the proposed mechanism for the thermal formation of CD, these are formed when the individual organic molecules from the raw material are condensed, among others, through amidation, aldol condensation, and radicals reaction, which produce larger chain intermediates, which reacts to form carbon clusters [3]. These clusters are carbonized due to the high temperature, and the remaining organic molecules passivate the resulting CD's surface [5,19,60,61]. For this reason, the differences between the particles' sizes can be attributed to the differences in the organic molecules present in the raw materials. Interestingly, the mentioned mechanism and TEM results suggest that this study's obtained materials have different surface compositions.

FTIR spectra are shown in Fig. 3 . All show broad bands with a maximum of wave number around 3500 to 3300 cm−1 attributable to O–H stretching vibrations of surface hydroxyl groups or adsorbed water and sharp bands at 2907 and 2870 cm−1 due to the stretching vibrations of the C–H groups (from –CH2 or –CH3). Peaks at 1250 and 1139 cm−1 may be assigned to C–O–C vibrations, and that at 1399 cm−1 demonstrates the presence of surface –C-OH vibrations of either carboxyl groups or tertiary alcohols in graphene oxides [62]. The presence of carboxyl groups is also supported by the peaks at 1734 and 1033 cm−1 attributable to CO and C–OH vibrations [62]. However, C–N groups' presence should not be discarded due to the possibility of overlapping with carboxylic groups around 1400 cm−1 [63]. The signal at 1610 cm−1 may be assigned to CC stretching in substituted alkenes and α and β unsaturated ketones, among others, as well as to HO vibrations of adsorbed water molecules [63]. Altogether, the FTIR spectra indicate that all the particles are highly oxidized. Specifically, CD-YM and CD-OP present abundant carboxyl groups, while CD-AS show abundant unsaturated aliphatic –like structures.

Raman spectroscopy is a useful technique to confirm the presence of graphitic structures in CD. Two characteristic bands in the ranges of ID: (1340–1400 cm−1) and IG: (1570–1700 cm−1) represent the vibration modes of carbon atoms in sp3 and sp2 hybridized orbitals, respectively. Specifically, the G-band is characteristic of graphitic layers, which is demonstrated because of the tangential vibration of sp2 bonded carbon atoms, and the D-bands corresponds to disordered carbons and defects in the graphitic structure [64]. Fig. 4 shows the Raman spectra of all considered materials, normalized to the corresponding IG band. As may be observed visually, the ID/IG ratio strongly depends on the material, thus suggesting different graphitic degree decreasing in the order CD-OP > CD-YM > CD-AS. Interestingly, the CD-OP here obtained has a higher graphitic degree (or fewer edge-defects) than those obtained from the same raw material but involving a hydrothermal carbonization route (ID/IG = 0.6) [23].
Altogether, IR and Raman spectra suggest a heterofunctional surface in all materials, with CD-OP having the lowest surface oxidation degree regarding the graphitic domains.

ζ-potential profiles (Fig. 5 ) show the typical behavior of nanomaterials with oxidized surfaces [50] in line with the IR results (vide supra). At pH = 6.5, ζ-potentials values of (−14.4 ± 0.7), (−18.7 ± 0.5), and (−10.3 ± 0.7) mV were obtained for CD-YM, CD-AS, and CD- OP, respectively, in line with the reported values of CD obtained from several raw materials [65]. Over the pH range studied, CD-AS showed the highest ζ-potential value. Figure. S2 shows a graphical procedure to estimate the pKa values of the surface acid groups on CD-AS through the ζ-potential vs. pH profiles [66]. All three materials have two apparent pKa in the pH range studied. The first one is between 3.5 and 5.0, attributed to surface carboxylic acid moieties. In this range, CD-AS suffers the highest degree of deprotonation, which results in the increase of negative values of ζ-potential from (−14.5 ± 0.6) to (−18.6 ± 0.6) mV, which might be due to the relative higher –COOH surface groups concentration when compared to the other materials. For the second apparent pKa, the range is different for each case, which might be due to the three original raw materials' different chemical compositions. Nevertheless, for all cases, this range's minimum value is 7.0, thus suggesting the presence of surface phenolic-like groups in different environments [67]. In that regard, CD-AS might be the material with the lowest surface concentration of these compounds, in line with the less intense IR peak at ca. 3500–3300 cm−1 due to HO stretching mode (vide supra).

Figure. S3 shows the UV/Vis spectra of the three obtained CD which presents the typical characteristics of CD, with contributions of π-π∗, n-π∗ and surface state transitions evidenced in the wavelength region of 220–260 and 270–330 and λ > 330 nm, respectively [68,69]. Furthermore, the energy gaps (EG) were calculated from Tauc plots considering a directly allowed semiconductor transition and small particle sizes [70].

The excitation-emission matrixes (EEM) of CD-OP, CD-YM, and CD-AS are shown in Fig. 6 and Figure S4, respectively. CD-OP and CD-YM show similar excitation-emission spectra and similar emission intensity for identical absorbance. Between the experimental error, EG = 3.80 eV for both particles. The transition involved in the energy gap corresponds to an excitation wavelength of 323 nm, which yields the emission spectrum with the highest intensity. For λex > 350 nm, an excitation wavelength emission dependence is observed—both the spectra maximum and shape changes when increasing the excitation wavelength. For the first parameter, a red-shift (movement to higher emission wavelength) is observed; meanwhile, the full width at half maximum (FWHM) decreases. This behavior has been reported for CD obtained from glucose through an acidic hydrothermal route [70]. Regarding CD-AS, a value of EG = 3.05 eV was obtained, and the excitation wavelength dependence is observed in all the emission range.

The fluorescence quantum yields, Φfl (λex = 350 nm) in air-saturated water at pH = 7.0 are (0.82 ± 0.10), (0.36 ± 0.04) and (0.07 ± 0.02) for CD-OP, CD-YM, and CD-AS, respectively. These values are in line with the high quantum yields reported in the literature for most CDs [71,72].

Photogeneration of reactive oxygen species (ROS) mediated by CD is a promising propriety due to its applicability in technological applications in the environment and nanomedicine. Steady-state generation and quenching of 1O2 are summarized in Figure. S5. Irradiation of air-saturated aqueous suspension of all CD (black curves) show no O2 depletion, which suggests the absence of chemical reactions between oxygen (in the ground or excited states) with the CD surfaces here considered. Additionally, complementary experiments with 1O2, specifically quenchers such as AF (chemical quencher) and NaN3 (physical quencher) with rate constant of 1.8 × 108 s−1M−1 y 4.5 × 108 s−1M−1, respectively, were done. Upon the addition of 10−3 M of FA, different behavior is observed depending on the sample. For CD-YM and CD-OP, it leads to a decrease in the O2 concentration upon irradiation (green curves). Thus, strongly supporting that upon irradiation at 350 nm, CD-YM and CD-OP may produce 1O2. Supporting the previous hypothesis, the simultaneous addition of FA and N3 − into the system leads to a competition between FA and N3 − for 1O2, which results in a decrease in the depletion rate of O2 as observed in the respective red curves. Regarding CD-AS, upon the addition of FA, the O2 depletion is also observed, but in the FA/N3 − containing systems, the depletion rate remains unperturbed (The slops of the corresponding lines are equal between the experimental error). Thus, considering that in the studied conditions, [N3 −] > [FA] and the respective 1O2 quenching constants: k (N3 −/1O2)> k (FA/1O2), the results suggest that CD-AS does not show the capacity of photosensitizing 1O2. Meanwhile, CD-YM and CD-OP seem to be suitable photosensitizers for producing this ROS. However, these quantum yields have not been calculated, and 1O2 production would be different. In that regard, the literature suggests that the defects and free radicals on CD's surface are responsible for producing singlet oxygen [72]. Interestingly, CD-AS, which is the only material considered in this study that cannot produce singlet oxygen, is the material with fewer edge-defects (vide supra).

Regarding superoxide radical anion (O2 .−) generation, since O2 .− recombines to produce H2O2, a colorimetric enzymatic test was used to measure the H2O2 concentration upon irradiating (λEx = 350 ± 20 nm) aqueous CD suspensions with the same absorbance at 350 nm. The measured [H2O2] were (0.25 ± 0.02), (0.47 ± 0.05), and (2.72 ± 0.01) μM/s for CD-OP, CD-YM, and CD-AS. Since O2 .− might react with the surface of the nanomaterials competing with the production of H2O2, the estimated O2 .− photoproduction is a lower limit value. Interestingly, CD-AS does not have the capacity to act as a singlet oxygen photosensitizer but can photogenerate micromolar concentrations of superoxide per second, thus stressing irradiated CD-AS capacity undergoing charge transfer to oxygen.

Methylene blue (MB) was selected as a probe molecule because of its UV/vis spectroscopy features. At the studied conditions, in the excitation range from (350 ± 20) nm, the absorbance of the CD materials is higher than that of the dye, as demonstrated in Fig. 7 -top left for the CD-YM/MB system. Marked spectral changes are demonstrated for MB when in contact with CD, been the most relevant the maxima red-shift to higher wavelengths, from 665 of the pure MB to 678 nm (Fig. 7- top right). Similar behavior has been reported for this dye when interacting with Na-montmorillonite [73]. In this study, the authors suggest the formation of surface MB-dimers mediated by electrostatic interactions between the surface and the dye. The absorption maxima position was suggested to depend on the degree of surface coverage by the dye. Herein, all CD have a net negative surface charge (vide supra), which might electrostatically interact with the dye due to its cationic nature facilitating its adsorption. This result might suggest an application field to CD in the design of nano-adsorbents to capture cationic species. Nevertheless, due to the graphitic contribution in the CD core, π-π interactions between the surface and the dye cannot be despised.

Photodegradation curves are shown in Fig. 7-bottom. Under the considered conditions, color depletion of around 15% is observed in the absence of CDs. MB has been reported to degrade under ultraviolet A (UVA) photolysis [74]. The proposed mechanism is pH-dependent, and the ROS photoproduced by the dye plays an important role, specially 1O2 [75,76].

Different MB degradation efficiencies are observed in the presence of identical amounts of either CD-AS, CD-OP, and CD-YM; CD-OP shows no statistical difference from the CD-free system. In parallel, CD-AS shows a color depletion of around 45% in 2 h. The faster decoloration was obtained using CD-YM, achieving almost 100% in 2 h. To elucidate the participation of 1O2, decoloration yields were re-evaluated under the presence of AF and sodium azide (NaN3) (Fig. 7-bottom right and Figure S7). Upon the addition of both quenchers, a decrease in the decoloration rate is observed for CD-YM, thus confirming that the MB photodecoloration occurs through a Type II mechanism involving singlet oxygen photosensitized by this material.

For the CD-OP/MB system, upon the addition of both 1O2 quenchers, the decoloration rate is even slower than the CD-free system, which confirms the inability of CD-OP to photodegrade MB. However, the contribution of other photodegradation pathways induced by direct MB photolysis, involving the generation of some oxidizing radicals as HO• or some organic radicals must be considered [75,77].

Interestingly, for CD-AS, the photodecoloration profile remains almost constant upon the addition of the singlet oxygen quenchers; this might suggest that this ROS is not responsible for the photodegradation of MB photocatalyzed by CD-AS (See Figure. S7). Thus, a different mechanism is involved comparing to the previously mentioned, and it might be due to the participation of superoxide radical anion. To prove that hypothesis, nitrotetrazolium blue chloride, a well-known superoxide scavenger [78], was introduced into the system, and the degradation yields decreased (Figure. S7). A marked yield reduction is observed when 33% v/v of ethanol, a non-selective radical quencher, was introduced into the system, suggesting other radicals' participation as the HO• or organic radicals generated by the interaction of organic matter in the CD-AS surface with the radiation [68,79].

Table S1 shows the MB discoloration founded in the literature for different discoloration systems related to the reported in this work. The discoloration yields reported are generally lower than values achieved for the YM-CD in this study [77,80–83]. For the cases with comparable or higher yields, they require more complex systems, the addition of harmful reactants, or shorter irradiation wavelength. It should be noted that for the MB removal data for each work, some assumptions were made, because most of the works follow the MB through UV/Vis spectroscopy, the final discoloration reported don't take into account the possibility that degradation products absorb in the same region that the MB. Likewise, for the MB removal on this work, an amount of catalyst of 10 mg was assumed in order to facilitate the comparison of processes. This exercise helps us to understand the advantages that the use of CD as catalyst would have and the importance of predicting their possible catalytic performance from its characterization.

The production of both ROS by photoexcitation of several CD have been reported [84,85]. Based on the results discussed above, different MB photodegradation mechanisms are responsible for the CD-free, CD-YM, and CD-AS systems (Fig. 8 ). For MB photolysis, the photoproduction of 1O2 by the dye itself has been reported [86]. Regarding the CD-YM photocatalyzed system, the participation of 1O2. The reaction between the molecular oxygen and the excited state of the CD-YM might produce this ROS. For CD-AS, due to the relatively low EBG (3.05 eV) and the λEXC = (350 ± 20) nm used in this study, the photoformation of the electron-hole pair is proposed, which supports both superoxide and hydroxyl radicals' participation [13].

Preliminary experiments showed that all materials could stabilize only oil/water emulsions when the oily phase is aromatic (not shown). CD's proposed mechanism as emulsion stabilizer suggests an active surface that facilitates π-π interactions between the aromatic molecules in the oily phase and H-bond interactions between the particles surface and the water molecules [17]. Fig. 9 shows a photograph in which the considered CD stabilizes bromobenzene-water emulsions. Nevertheless, from a kinetic perspective, all materials have different performance. CD-OP stabilized emulsions break between 30 min after its formation. Meanwhile, the emulsions stabilized with CD-AS and CD-YM are stable for more than three days.

Fig. 9 also shows the temporal changes of the mean droplet size for 30 min. It is evident how the mean droplet sizes increase with time for CD-OP, demonstrating the droplets' coalescence. In fact, in the photograph in the same picture, CD-OP emulsion is the only one in which phase separation is observed. This suggests that CD-OP is not able to stabilize bromobenzene-water emulsions in contrast to CD-AS ad CD-YM systems, in which the size of the droplets remains almost constant in the considered period, and no emulsion break is demonstrated in the photograph. According to the Raman results, while CD-OP is the most graphitic structured nanoparticle but with the highest negative surface charge, CD-AS and CD-YM showed higher content of defects and less negatively charged interactions with the oily and aqueous liquids simultaneously. Thus, from all materials herein considered, CD-AS and CD-YM are promising as aromatic oily phase-water emulsion stabilizers due to their structural (amphiphilic) properties.

Yerba mate, orange peel, and avocado seeds are useful raw materials for obtaining carbon dots of less than 10 nm mean diameter and polyfunctional surfaces through a simple pyrolysis route, with marked different physicochemical properties. The pyrolysis temperature was fixed between those of cellulose and lignin thermal decompositions. Although the same pyrolysis temperature was used for all the cases, CDs with different physicochemical properties, percentage of graphitic structures, and oxidation grades were obtained depending on precursors, as summarized in Table 1 .

The experimental results suggest that Raman spectroscopy is a powerful technique to characterize CD and the ID/IG parameter is a useful first approximation of these material's performances in photocatalysis and Pickering emulsion stabilization.

CD-OP has a predominant graphitic structure with a highly oxidized surface. It shows the highest fluorescent capacity (Φem = 82%). Although it can photosensitize singlet oxygen, the yield photodegradation of MB catalyzed by this material is low, probably due to a relatively low singlet oxygen quantum yield. It is neither a good surfactant for Pickering emulsion stabilization purposes. Therefore, the high luminescence quantum-yield and negligible singlet oxygen production facilitate CD-OP use as fluorescence tracers [87].

CD-AS structures present a high proportion of sp3 C, thus supporting defects on the graphitic structure, suggesting abundant unsaturated aliphatic–like structures with low fluorescence quantum yields (Φem = 7%), though it can photosensitize superoxide anion radical and, therefore, catalyze MB degradation. It is, however, a good surfactant for bromobenzene-water emulsion stabilization.

CD-YM shows an intermediate situation with graphitic and defected domains [88,89], and abundant negative surface charges, mainly consisting of carboxylate groups. Its fluorescence quantum yield is intermediate between the other synthesized materials but shows the capacity for singlet oxygen photosensitization and the highest efficiency for MB degradation involving a singlet-oxygen mechanism. It also shows excellent performance as a Pickering emulsion stabilizer. The observed singlet oxygen photosensitizing capacity is of interest in wastewater treatment.

In general, the materials herein synthesized are potential, self-sustaining, environmentally friendly, and with the possibility of application in different fields depending on the initial raw material, and the study of their physicochemical characteristics it is a great approximation toward the prediction of possible applications for CD materials based on vegetable biomass.
